coredata.dc:title: Influence of hydrophilic carbon dots on polyamide thin film nanocomposite reverse osmosis membranes
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yi
Li, Sha
Zhang, Kaisong
coredata.dc:description: There is critical to fabricate reverse osmosis (RO) membranes with high water-solute selectivity, thereby decreasing the energy input required in desalination processes. To achieve this, a super hydrophilic nano Carbon Dots (CDs) with quantum size of 6.8nm was self-synthesized and successfully incorporated into the selective layer of polyamide (PA) thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) RO membrane. The novel CDs-TFN RO membrane was fabricated by dispersing the CDs nanoparticles at varying quantities into aqueous solution through interfacial polymerization (IP) of monomers of m-phenylenediamine (MPD) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC). Using Excitation and emission matrix (EEM) to explore the incorporation of CDs into the membrane skin layer, we observe that the fluorescence properties of CDs-TFN membrane decreased in order of increasing CDs concentration in relation to the unmodified TFC RO membrane which has the highest fluorescence count. The surface morphology of all TFN membranes transform from a leaf-like structure into ridge-and-valley structure with increased roughness, which has an important implication for flux enhancement. The TFN membrane with 0.02wt% CDs incorporation exhibited promising desalination performance with water flux of 87.1L/m2/h and salt rejection of 98.8% than the control thin film composite (TFC) membrane (2000ppm NaCl solution, 25°C, 1.55MPa). The improved performances of CDs-TFN membrane were attributed to the enhanced hydrophilicity, decreased thickness as well as the increased density of the selective layer due to the incorporation of small amounts of CDs nanoparticles. By extending the deposition time of CDs on the polysulfone substrate, the membrane performance was further improved with water flux of 88.7L/m2/h and salt rejection of 99.0%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Thin-film nanocomposite
Reverse osmosis membrane
Interfacial polymerization
Brackish water desalination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of CDs on surface morphological characteristics
Excitation-emission spectra of CDs in TFN membranes
Effects of CDs content on TFN membrane desalination performance
Effects of deposition time on TFN membrane desalination performance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic illustration of the preparation procedure of CDs by hydrothermal carbonization of glucose.
The schematic process of CDs-TFN membrane fabrication.
Characterization of CDs using (a) TEM image and (b) corresponding particle size distribution histogram of as prepared CDs.
FTIR spectra of CDs (in red line) and glucose (in blue line). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Effects of CDs loading on pure water contact angles of TFC and TFN membranes.

SEM images for the surfaces of (a) TFC membrane and (b-f) TFN membrane with CDs loading (0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.08, 0.08wt%, respectively) under ×10,000 (a1-e1), ×5000 (f1), and ×100,000 (a2-f2) magnification, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

SEM images for the cross section morphologies of (a) TFC membrane and (b-e) TFN membranes with CDs loading (0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.08wt%, respectively) under 20,000× magnification. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Three dimensional AFM images of TFC membrane (a) and CDs-TFN membrane prepared by embedding CDs nanoparticles in the aqueous solution with different contents. (b) 0.01wt% CDs-TFN, (c) 0.02wt% CDs-TFN, (d) 0.05wt% CDs-TFN, (e) 0.08wt% CDs-TFN.
The graphical model for the interaction of the hydrophilic CDs nanoparticles during the interfacial polymerization.

EEM fluorescence spectra of (a-e) TFC and CDs-TFN membranes, (f) Changes of PL emission spectra of the TFN membranes with an increase in CDs modification measured at λex=328nm and (g) plot of excitation decay for TFC and TFN membranes with increase of CDs loading.
Effects of CDs content on water flux and salt rejection (adding CDs nanoparticles in aqueous solution; 2000ppm NaCl solution, 225psi).
Effects of deposition time on water flux and salt rejection (adding CDs nanoparticles in aqueous solution; 2000ppm NaCl solution, 225psi).
Surface roughness parameters of TFC and CDs-TFN membranes.
Desalination performance comparison of pristine TFC, CDs-TFN membranes, commercial RO membrane and other high performance TFN RO membranes.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Dow-BW 30-commerical brackish water RO membrane; all membranes were tested under 225psi (15.5bar) pressure with 2000ppm NaCl in the feed.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: “A” represents nano-filler in aqueous phase; “O” represents nano-filler in organic phase.

head.ce:title: Influence of hydrophilic carbon dots on polyamide thin film nanocomposite reverse osmosis membranes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Urban Pollutant Conversion, Institute of Urban Environment, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Xiamen 361021, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Urban Pollutant Conversion, Institute of Urban Environment, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Biomaterials of Guangdong Higher Education Institutes, Department of Biomedical Engineering, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Biomaterials of Guangdong Higher Education Institutes, Department of Biomedical Engineering, Jinan University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: There is critical to fabricate reverse osmosis (RO) membranes with high water-solute selectivity, thereby decreasing the energy input required in desalination processes. To achieve this, a super hydrophilic nano Carbon Dots (CDs) with quantum size of 6.8nm was self-synthesized and successfully incorporated into the selective layer of polyamide (PA) thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) RO membrane. The novel CDs-TFN RO membrane was fabricated by dispersing the CDs nanoparticles at varying quantities into aqueous solution through interfacial polymerization (IP) of monomers of m-phenylenediamine (MPD) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC). Using Excitation and emission matrix (EEM) to explore the incorporation of CDs into the membrane skin layer, we observe that the fluorescence properties of CDs-TFN membrane decreased in order of increasing CDs concentration in relation to the unmodified TFC RO membrane which has the highest fluorescence count. The surface morphology of all TFN membranes transform from a leaf-like structure into ridge-and-valley structure with increased roughness, which has an important implication for flux enhancement. The TFN membrane with 0.02wt% CDs incorporation exhibited promising desalination performance with water flux of 87.1L/m2/h and salt rejection of 98.8% than the control thin film composite (TFC) membrane (2000ppm NaCl solution, 25°C, 1.55MPa). The improved performances of CDs-TFN membrane were attributed to the enhanced hydrophilicity, decreased thickness as well as the increased density of the selective layer due to the incorporation of small amounts of CDs nanoparticles. By extending the deposition time of CDs on the polysulfone substrate, the membrane performance was further improved with water flux of 88.7L/m2/h and salt rejection of 99.0%.

ce:section.ce:para: Progressively increasing world's population and expanding development of agriculture and industry continues to pose enormous challenge on water resources which is crucial to the survival of human beings and the development of world economies [1]. Considering the high process-ability, low cost, as well as the flexibility of membrane systems, reverse osmosis（RO）is currently the most widespread desalination technology for producing high-quality water from sea water, brackish water and non-traditional water sources [2,3].

Thin-film-composite (TFC) polyamide (PA) membranes dominate the RO membrane market for water desalination, and are typically fabricated using monomers of m-phenylene-diamine (MPD) and monomers of trimesoyl chloride (TMC) through interfacial polymerization [3,4]. However, the highly cross-linked networks and hydrophobic aromatic materials inherently limit water flux efficiency [5]. Moreover, permeability-selectivity trade-off issues for nonporous polymeric membranes deters RO membrane separation performance [6]. This implies that a higher water permeation will often result in a lower salt rejection. Meanwhile, a highly selective membrane is of great significance in improving water quality and eliminating additional separation stages in water desalination, which if successful will represent an important advancement in next-generation RO membrane [7]. Therefore, there is a current pressing need for fabrication of membranes with high water- solute selectivity properties [8].

In 2007, Jeong et al. [9] were the first to report the concept of thin-film-nanocomposite (TFN) membrane by adding hydrophilic NaA zeolite nanoparticles into the matrix of PA films, which opens up a new research direction for the modification of membranes. The newly developed TFN membrane exhibited higher water flux and maintained a steady salt rejection. Recently Kah et al. [3] proposed the use of nanocomposite membranes, which offers the merits of increased flux and improved desalination. Moreover, considerable number of research have been dedicated to introducing functional nanoparticles into polymeric membranes, such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [10–12], graphene oxide [13,14], silica [15–17], TiO2 [18], zeolite [9,19,20], alumina [21], metal-organic frameworks [22] and POSS [23,24]. The detailed nanofiller properties and relative desalination performances of the modified TFN membranes are summarized in Table S1.

Matrix-particle compatibility plays a key role in the incorporation of nanofillers into PA membrane to improve the membrane desalination performances [14]. In a more recent research, Duan et al. [22] incorporated hydrophobic metal-organic framework (MOF) ZIF-8 into PA layer of TFN membrane. Compared with classic hydrophilic zeolite, the ZIF-8 exhibited better compatibility due to the imidazolate linker addition, which increases compatibility between the inorganic ZIF-8 and organic PA matrix. The resultant membranes showed 162% increased water permeability than the pristine membrane but maintained NaCl rejection. Multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWNTs) are intrinsically hydrophobic and have been employed in the fabrication of TFN RO membrane by acidifying the MWNTs, which showed a slightly improved dispersion in MPD solution [25]. The as-prepared membranes achieved increase in water flux with an accompanying decrease in salt rejection. Other works were conducted to increase the compatibility of the MWNTs in the PA layer by grafting with abundant hydrophilic groups [11]. A significant water flux improvement was obtained without forfeiting the solute rejection. Besides, the particle size offers an additional degree of freedom to tailor the membrane permeability, selectivity, structure, morphology as well as the interface of TFN membranes [26]. Undoubtedly, the emergence of nanotechnology provides an attractive and facial alternative to improve membrane performance [27]. However, most TFN membranes showed a significant enhancement in water permeability at the expense of marginal decrease or maintained selectivity as summarized above. Particle size determines a defect-free membrane performance as observed by Mary L. Lind [26]. Larger sized particles especially impermeable inorganic nanoparticles easily produce non-selective voids formed between the layers of the PA skin [18]. Smaller sized nanoparticles produce greater enhancements in membrane permeability and salt rejection [26]. Therefore, incorporation of more polymer compatible and smaller dimension nano-materials is an ongoing research prospect for improving PA TFN membrane desalination performances.

As an emerging class of carbonaceous nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have drawn tremendous attention since its first synthesis in 2004 [28]. Due to their low toxicity, excellent water dispersibility, ease of synthesis and functionalization as well as their photo stability, they are have been explored intensively for their potential application in bioimaging, biosensing and other biological related fields [29–31]. Carbon dots are luminescent nanoparticles with nanosize generally less than 10nm, and can be produced in large-scale through a wide range of carbon sources using inexpensive facial approaches [30,32]. The size and surface properties of CDs nanoparticles can be easily tuned by changing the raw material or reaction parameters [33,34]. The amount of functional groups on the carbon dots such as carboxyl, carbonyl, hydroxyl, amino and other oxygenous groups makes it highly soluble and biocompatible [29,31]. For this reason, incorporation of CDs into polymeric membrane may be a plausible approach. Carbonaceous materials, such as CDs have high affinity for polyamide materials, therefore hydrophilic CDs not only can be utilized to enhance the hydrophilicity of membrane surface but also to tailor the morphology, roughness, thickness and density of polyamide structure. Consequently, these unique properties and advantages make CDs a promising material for TFN reverse osmosis membrane modification.

To our knowledge, there is no report about the incorporation of carbon dots into polyamide TFC membrane. Herein, a super hydrophilic carbon dots with nanosize of 6.8nm applied as nanofiller in TFN membranes for improved water desalination. In this paper, we utilize this special class of carbon material, which is also an environmental friendly nanoparticle to structurally modify the selective layer of the polyamide membrane to improve the flux and enhance/stabilize the membrane selectivity. We hypothesized that the abundant hydrophilic functional groups on the CDs could 1) enhanced the solubility and diffusivity of MPD molecules during interfacial polymerization producing a slightly dense PA interface, which speculatively may be as a result of the slight increase in interfacial tension and solubility difference and thus, provides an important implication for solute selectivity of the novel nano-enhanced membrane; 2) create an intrinsic pathway to enhance the membrane permeability and also impact the salt rejection through repulsive charge production; and 3) increase the roughness of the surface morphology thereby expanding the wettability of the outermost support layer. The effects of CDs loading concentrations and deposition time on the membrane separation performance were also investigated.

The microporous polysulfone support (PSf) with nonwoven polyester fabric was fabricated in the lab through phase inversion. The active polyamide layer of the RO membrane was formed by interfacial polymerization of monomers of m-phenylenediamine (MPD, 99%, Sigma- Aldrich) in aqueous phase and trimesoyl chloride (TMC, 98%, Sigma- Aldrich) in organic phase reaction. Besides, chemicals in aqueous phase included triethylamine (TEA, 99.5+%, Sigma- Aldrich), (+)-10-camphorsulfonic acid (CSA, 99%, Sigma- Aldrich) and Isopar G® (ExxonMobil Chemical) used as solvent in organic phase. Raw materials used for the synthesis of the Carbon-dots included D-(+)-Glucose anhydrous (C6H12O6, >99.7%, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd), Potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4, >99.7%, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd) and ethanol (C2H6O, >99.7%, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd), which was used for precipitation of free salt. The membrane permeability and rejection performance were tested using sodium chloride (NaCl, >99.7%, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd). All the chemicals were used as received. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout the experiments. The BW30 membrane was kindly provided by Dow Water & Process Solutions.

In this work, the hydrothermal treatment step using glucose as substrate is similar to that reported by Zheng et al. [35] and is as follows: 1.0g of glucose and 1090mg of KH2PO4 were dissolved in 40mL of deionized water. The solution mixture was transferred into a 100mL Teflon-lined autoclave and purged with N2 for 1h to remove O2 dissolved in the solution. After hydrothermal reaction in the oven at 473K for 12h, the resulting brown precipitates were recovered by centrifugation at 7830rpm for 15min and filtered through a 0.22µm filter. The yellow supernatant was collected and freeze dried. The obtained fine milky powder was re-dispersed in ethanol through vortexing to precipitate the free salt. The yellow ethanol solution was further filtered through PTFE syringe filter with pore size of 0.22µm and rotary evaporation was conducted to remove ethanol gently. The muciform CDs obtained were kept in dryer for further characterization and use. It should be noted that the obtained liquid-like CDs nanoparticles were sticky and therefore restricts the complete elimination of ethanol by the rotary evaporation process. This residual trace of ethanol bonded tightly together by hydrogen bond carries hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups on the surface of CDs, which resulted in a dark brown dope ( Fig. 1).

The CDs-TFN RO membrane was fabricated by interfacial polymerization on the PSf membrane. The support PSf membrane was cut and taped onto a frame, and then immersed in DI water for 12h to remove the NaHSO3 and allowed to dry under ambient temperature for 3min before use. The PSf support layer was subsequently immersed in aqueous solution of MPD containing CDs (0, 0.01, 0.02, 0.05, and 0.08wt% each). The as-prepared MPD solution with CDs dispersed exhibited a remarkable stability even without sonication as shown in Fig. S1 (a), (b). Excess solution was removed with a rubber roller and dried by erecting it vertically. The prepared PSf membrane was immersed in Isopar G® organic solvent containing TMC to form a PA layer. The resulting membrane was drained and dried in oven at 363K for further cross-linking reaction and solvent evaporation. Finally, the treated membrane was immediately soaked in DI water for storage before testing. The resultant CDs-TFN membranes (that is, the TFC membrane made using MPD solution containing CDs nanoparticles) showed a uniform light brown color as is shown in Fig. S1 (c). Fig. 2 depicts the incorporation of CDs nanoparticles into the PA-TFN membrane.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed with H-7650, Hitachi, Japan operating at 100kV accelerating voltage to study the morphological structure of synthesized CDs particles. The CDs solution was premixed with DI water and diluted severally prior to dropping on the Copper network. The size and distribution of CDs nanoparticles were analyzed using a computer program “Image J”. UV–vis absorption of the nanoparticles was conducted by an ultraviolet and visible spectrophotometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu). Photoluminescence [28] emission measurements were carried out to study the fluorescence properties of CDs using a F-4600 fluorescence spectorophotomet (F-4600, HITACHI). X-Ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the as-prepared samples were measured using X′ Pert PRO. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of the synthesized CDs was also acquired using an FTIR spectrophotometer (Nicolet iS10, Thermo Scientific, USA) with a mass of dried CDs particles.

The prepared membranes were rinsed and dried for 24h in oven at 303K before being characterized. The membrane surface chemical structure were examined using FTIR spectrum on a Nicolet iS10 (Thermo Fisher Scientific) equipped with multi-reflection Smart Performer ATR accessory. The contact angles were measured with a 5.0-µL DI water drop using the sessile drop method on a video contact angle system (DSA100, German KRUSS). The membrane surface and cross-sectional morphology were carried out using a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, HITACHI S-4800) to determine the superficial details of the ridge and valley structure modified by CDs content. The surface morphologies and roughness of prepared membranes were observed by atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Aglient, USA) with taping mode measurements in air. Attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscope (Thermo nicoler Avatar 360 FTIR spectrometer) was used to identify the functional groups embedded in the CDs-TFN membranes. Finally, fluorescence spectorophotomet(F-4600, HITACHI) for three-dimensional excitation-emission matrix (EEM) was adopted to confirm the incorporation of CDs in the membranes.

A lab-scale cross-flow membrane permeation cell made of stainless-steel material was employed to evaluate the desalination performance of the prepared RO membranes. The effective testing membrane area was 42cm2. The transport measurement was conducted at trans-membrane pressure (drive force) 225psi (1.55MPa), under ambient temperature controlled by the water cooling system. NaCl solution of 2000ppm was prepared as the feed solution to simulate brackish water.

The water flux was calculated by measuring the volume of the permeate flow over a period of time using Eq. (1-1):
(1-1)
J =
Q
A t
where J is the permeate water flux (L/m2 h), Q is the volume of collected permeate water (L) during a time interval t (h), and A is the effective area of the testing membrane (m2).

The desalinization performance or membrane selectivity was calculated by measuring the differences of electrical conductivity between the feed and permeate water using a portable conductivity meter (sensION+EC5, HACH) and computed by the following Eq. (1–2):
(1−2)
R ( % ) =
(
1
-
c
p
c
f
)
× 100
where
c
p
and
c
f
represent salt concentration in the permeate and feed solutions, respectively.

The morphology and size distribution of the synthesized CDs nanoparticles analyzed by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) are illustrated in Fig. 3. The image shown in Fig. 3(a) demonstrated well dispersed and spherical dots-like nanoparticles. The hydrogen-bonding interactions among the hydroxyl groups of adjacent CDs nanoparticles shortened the distance between these nano CDs [33]. The size distribution assessment indicated that the as-prepared CDs were narrowly distributed and the average size was determined to be 6.79±1.65nm (Fig. 3b). We controlled the mole ratio of glucose/KH2PO4 in 7:10, hence, the larger size of CDs particles obtained in our study [35]. A typical X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of CDs in Fig. S2 (a) showed a wide peak at 23.87°, which indicates an amorphous structure [36].

The absorption and emission spectra (Fig. S2 (b)) of the synthesized CDs displayed a strong fluorescence [35]. As depicted in Fig. S2 (c), PL assessment of the emission peaks for CDs show that the dispersion shifted to longer wavelengths with increasing excitation wavelengths from 325nm to 500nm in 25nm increments. It was assumed that the phenomenon λex-dependent λem originated from the different surface defects introduced by the abundant functional groups during the hydrothermal carbonization process. These surface defects act as excitation energy traps and resulted in the different PL properties [36].

As presented in Fig. 4, the typical bands (1462, 1151, 1102, 1027, 919 and 841cm−1) of glucose were absent in the FTIR spectra of CDs [35]. The vibration band of –OH (3440cm−1), C˭O (1640cm−1) confirmed the presence of hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of the CDs nanoparticles [35]. Besides, the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration bands of C–O–C at 1350 and 1200cm−1, respectively indicated that the surface of CDs is partially oxidized [35]. The above observations from FTIR spectrum confirm that the synthesized CDs nanoparticles is abundant with hydroxyl and carbonyl/carboxyl moieties which may originate from the hydrothermal carbonization of glucose [35].

As is shown in Fig. 5. The contact angle of the TFC membrane decreased consistently with the CDs content from 0.01wt% to 0.08wt%. However, there was only a little reduction in contact angle at CDs loading concentration less than 0.02wt%. This may be due to loss of CDs particles (at exceptionally low particle content) after the aqueous reaction. At higher CDs loading, a linear decrease in contact angle for the CDs-TFN membrane (from 78.6° to 63.2°) was observed. Generally, decline of contact angle indicates increase of hydrophilic property for TFC membrane, which in turn results in higher water permeability [20]. The decrement of the CDs-TFN membrane contact angle may be ascribed to the abundant negatively charged hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surfaces of CDs. Therefore, CDs incorporation plays a great role in improving the hydrophilicity of TFN membrane surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs on surface morphological characteristics
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphological features of TFC and TFN membranes with varying CDs content are presented in Fig. 6. All the membranes displayed a characteristic ridge-and-valley structure, which is typical for polyamide membrane synthesized via interfacial polymerization of MPD and TMC [37]. Compared to the unmodified membrane, the CDs-TFN membranes seemed to exhibit a less leaf-like surface structure Fig. 6 [b-e]. In contrast, reports from some research have shown that the incorporation zeolite nanoparticles might result in a more “leaf-like” surface morphology [20]. Here, the embedding of CDs in the aqueous phase may have interfered with the interfacial polymerization reaction in a way different from the mechanism of reaction with the inorganic nanoparticles. In order to give a clear insight into these variations, higher magnification SEM images of the TFC and TFN membrane surface morphologies were presented in Fig. 6. As shown in a2, the TFC membrane displayed the leaf-like PA layer surface, which are vertically stack and piled up with less porosity. The nodular polyamide dense phase was close to the polysulfone side [37,38]. By contrast, the CDs-TFN membranes exhibited a more open “ridge-and-valley” structure of loose polyamide extending outward from the nodular dense phase. With the increase of CDs loading, the dense nodular polyamide uniformly spread and become more exposed to the surface which constitutes the main selective layer. The decreasing leaf-like polyamide layer provides more open space and facilitates water transport. In addition, the significant variations in surface morphology may be explained by the presence of nucleation which allows CDs nanodots to be embedded in the nucleus of the polyamide during the interfacial polymerization reaction.

Fig. 6 (f1) displayed the overwhelmingly assembly of CDs particles on the surface of TFN membrane in a 0.08wt% CDs loading. The red circle in Fig. 6 (f1) was marked around features believed to be the CDs nanoparticles. These visible round nano-dots particles clumped-up with size around 20nm, which appear slightly larger in the SEM image. The clumpy appearance might be due to the necessary metal coating to make the entire sample conductive. Despite the quantum nano size effect, the electrostatic interaction between the abundant surface oxygenic functional groups and hydrogen bonds made the CDs nanoparticles easy to coacervate.

As presented in Fig. 7, analysis of the SEM cross-sectional images showed marked morphological differences between the TFC membrane and TFN membranes in terms of the polymer density distribution. The pristine membranes showed a dense and homogeneous PA skin layer with thickness ranges from 179 to 288nm. By comparison, all CDs-TFN membranes exhibited an irregular and fluctuating ridge-and-valley surface structure, particularly at high CDs concentration. The TFN membrane with 0.02wt% CDs exhibited a relative thinner and dense barrier. The more open structure and thinner selective layer of CDs-TFN 0.02% improved the surface wettability via creation of water delivery pathway with shortened distance and less trans-membrane resistance [37]. However, a thick “crust” was developed between the selective layer and porous substrate, which constitute a unique triple layer configuration, high in CDs content. This portion is marked with the red rectangle in Fig. 7(e). In addition, CDs nanoparticles prepared in this work has a nano size of 6.8nm, which was larger than that of MPD molecules (kinetic diameter >5.9Å), and could be a mass transport media between the interface of aqueous solution and organic solution as depicted in the Fig. 9. The nanoparticles played two roles during the IP. On one hand, the hydrophilic CDs enlarged the region between the interface of aqueous solution and organic solution due to the hydrophilic properties of the functional groups which gave rise to the repulsion to the organic phase, and resulted in a thinner selective skin layer formed with a small amount of CDs nanoparticles. On the other hand, the solubility and diffusivity of MPD molecules were both enhanced by a large amount of hydrophilic CDs through hydrogen bond, which accelerate the kinetics of film formation by interfacial polymerization and led to the dense polyamide film formed. The hydrophilic groups (hydroxyl groups and carboxyl groups) functionalized on the nanoparticles can facilitate the molecule transportation, and play important role in assisting the diffusion of MPD to the interface. The enhanced MPD diffusivity also explained the decreased water flux and the observed crust layer formed.

The surface morphology and roughness parameters of the prepared RO membranes were also explored using AFM analysis. Fig. 8 shows three dimensional AFM images of the TFC and CDs-TFN RO membranes at a scan size of 5µm×5µm. The AFM clearly shows the significant differences between the surface morphologies of the prepared membranes. The CDs embedded TFN RO membrane exhibits relatively large-scale surface roughness of ridge-and-valley structures compared to the pristine membrane surface which is relatively smooth. Besides, Table 1 reveals the surface roughness parameters of TFC and CDs-TFN membranes. The average roughness (Ra) value of the pristine membrane increased gradually from 24.9nm to 63.7nm with increasing CDs loading in aqueous solution from zero to 0.05wt%. This could also be observed in ten point height of irregularities (Rz). The increase in surface roughness can be beneficial for water permeability by providing greater surface area available for water molecules to permeate [39]. It should be noted that the TFN membrane with 0.08wt% CDs incorporation exhibited a cubic like surface morphology and the Ra was reduced significantly to 33.5nm. This can be related to the aggregation of CDs nanoparticles as we observed in SEM surface morphology. The accumulated filler encapsulation could change the rate and the pathway of MPD diffusion, which led to higher resistance of formed films [22].

The increase in surface roughness upon addition of CDs nanoparticles is likely due to the interaction of CDs during the interfacial polymerization. The amount of hydrophilic groups on CDs nanoparticles enlarged the reaction region of two immiscible phases between aqueous phase and organic phase, which resulted in MPD molecules penetrate into the deep of organic phase and consequently formed the increased nodular and wrinkle morphology. A graphic model was presented in Fig. 9 to illustrate the mechanism of CDs interaction during the interfacial polymerization.

The combined use of multiple surface techniques (surface and cross sections) confirmed the presence of CDs in the PA skin layer. To further demonstrate the successful incorporation of CDs into the PA TFN membrane attenuated total reflectance infra-red (ATR-IR) spectroscopy was used to investigate the chemical structures of both the pristine and prepared CDs-TFN membranes, which produced ATR-IR spectra from 2400 to 3500cm−1, with all displaying almost similar spectra pattern as shown in Fig. S3. However, a characteristics peak of -OH was detected at ~3405cm−1 (Fig. S3 (a)). Compared with TFC membrane, there are gradient variations in absorption intensity as the concentration of CDs increased. As can be seen in Fig. S3 (b), the FTIR spectrum from 1500 to 1800cm−1 shows weak C˭C stretching band at 1610cm−1 and a strong C˭O band at 1700cm−1 which originate from the abundant carboxyl/carbonyl groups on the surface of CDs nanoparticles. The observed presence of these hydrophilic functional groups in the PA thin film layers forms an important implication for the successful incorporation of CDs in the prepared CDs-TFN membrane and thus explains the reason for the improved surface hydrophilicity of the modified membrane. Similarly, the absorbance intensity shows positive correlation with increment of CDs content. In addition, the surface elemental analysis for TFC and TFN membranes were measured as presented in the Table S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Excitation-emission spectra of CDs in TFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: To date no report has been performed with the three-dimensional excitation-emission matrix (EEM) fluorescence spectroscopy to characterize the binding of nanoparticles into PA membranes skin layer. In this work, detailed studies on the PL intensity of TFC and TFN membranes were performed to investigate the effects of CDs, as shown in Fig. 10 (a-e). Evidently, in Fig. 10 (a) the TFC membrane showed the highest and strongest fluorescence peak at excitation (Ex)/emission (Em) =λ328nm/λ382nm. The fluorescence of TFC membrane originated from the intrinsic fluorophore of polyamide and the rigid and conjugated structure of benzene ring whereas, the TFN membranes exhibited a shrinking photoluminescence peak area. That is, the intensity of the PL decreased with the increase of CDs embedded concentration. The emission spectra with the characteristic PL emission at 382nm decayed with incorporated CDs concentration excited at 328nm as shown in Fig. 10 (f). The PL decay plot (Fig. 10 (g)) exhibited positive correlation between CDs concentration and particularly at CDs loading concentration greater than 0.01wt%, with nearly linear dependency. This linearly reducing fluorescence intensity supports the hypothesis that not only does the CDs have a great effect on the photoluminescent characteristics but also the binding of CDs into the membrane to a large extent.

This phenomenon could be explained in two ways: firstly, the binding of CDs with PA skin layer was likely to induce the breakdown of polyamide and the displacement of the -COCl groups. The abundant functional groups from the CDs surfaces interact with polyamide through hydrogen bonding and nonspecific interaction, which loosen the chain-chain interactions. Secondly, the conjugated CDs could facilitate electron transfer and energy absorption due to its good conductivity and large surface area. The target CDs can easily quench the FL of TFC membrane by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [40]. This application can prove useful in the study of other nanomaterial incorporation into the thin film PA layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CDs content on TFN membrane desalination performance
ce:section.ce:para: As depicted in Fig. 11. at the 0.02wt% CDs loading, the TFN membrane showed the best separation performance with high flux (87.1L/m2/h, 2.09m3/m2/day) and improved salt rejection (98.8%). There was remarkable enhancement of the water flux by 22.0% and a little rise in salt rejection by 0.3% as compared with the TFC membrane (see Table S3). Notably, the decrease in water flux accompanied by high content of CDs nanoparticles (NPs) in the polyamide layer is in contrast with the results reported by other researchers where the water flux increased along with the nanoparticle content and decrease of salt rejection at slightly high NPs content [20,22]. This variation could be explained as the effect of hydrophilic CDs NPs, which improved the solubility and diffusivity of MPD molecules. Thus, this accelerated the transfer of MPD molecules to the interface during interfacial polymerization and subsequently forms a thicker polyamide crust layer with low permeability as earlier described. At the same time, the aggregation of NPs at high contents can easily create interstitial defects in PA TFN selective layer during fabrication process, which provides extra channels for salt ions. Besides, the effects of thickness on water permeation are likely to be more serious than that from the defects of aggregation of CDs NPs. Finally, it should be noted that the increased nanoparticle loading could also lead to the blocking of few pores on the substrate and this effect was more serious when loading concentrations was higher.

The results indicated that the CDs nanoparticles play a great role in the increase of water flux as well as salt rejection for CDs-TFN membrane. The improved performances of CDs-TFN membrane were attributed to the enhanced hydrophilicity, decreased thickness as well as the increased density of the selective layer due to the incorporation of small content of CDs nanoparticles. However, to confirm the competency of the CDs-TFN membrane, it is necessary to test the stability. Generally, most TFN membranes would likely suffer from leaching during long term operation process due to poor compatibility between the nano-filler and polymer matrix, as the case with Ag etc. However, because of the functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl, carboxyl, and epoxide) on CDs nanoparticles, the compatibility between the nanoparticles and the host (polymer) materials was greatly improved possibly through covalent or non-covalent interactions as shown in Fig. 9. Therefore, the hydrophilic CDs is believed to be incorporated and fixed into the polymer matrix, which can hardly wash away even in long term operation. Beside, long term stability test commonly performed to verify desalination performance of TFN membranes generally reaches stability after 2–3h [14,41]. In this work, we observed the membrane desalination performance stabilized after 2h (both salt rejection and water flux), except there was a slight decrease in water flux, which stabilized after 3h operation time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of deposition time on TFN membrane desalination performance
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the quantum nano size and imponderable weight of CDs, this class of NPs hardly adheres to the substrate tightly and is easily washed away along with the aqueous solution when draining the excess aqueous phase. Therefore, the effect of deposition time of CDs onto the substrate was explored by fixing the CDs content constant at 0.02wt% in this experiment with expected to favor the attachment of CDs to the PSf substrate. The water flux and salt rejection versus deposition time of CDs on substrate are shown in Fig. 12. The results indicated that the deposition time had an effect on the performance of water flux and salt rejection. At 6min deposition time, the CDs-TFN membrane showed the best separation performance in consideration of both the water flux (88.7L/m2/h) and salt rejection (99.0%). However, the longer deposition time aided the penetration of MPD molecules into the porous substrate, and consequently narrowing or blocking the pores due to increased polyamide formed within the pores during the interfacial polymerization and thereby decreasing the water flux. [42].

Table 2 compares the desalination performance of membranes prepared in this work with the commercial Dow-BW 30 membrane, and other high performance TFN membranes reported. All membranes were tested under 225psi hydraulic pressure with 2000ppm NaCl feed. The 0.02wt% CDs modified TFN membrane outperformed the Dow-BW30 membrane with 52.5% higher in water permeance (5.72L/m2 hbar vs. 3.68L/m2 hbar) and with a slight higher in salt rejection (99% vs. 98.1%). Generally, comparison between different membranes with a clear conclusion might be somewhat misleading. This is because, both recipe used and IP processes are not the same and the fabrication scale are dissimilar. However, it would be interesting to do further work regarding scale up of CDs-TFN in the very near future.

A fluorescent super hydrophilic CDs nanoparticles (<10nm) was systematically investigated in thin-film-nanocomposite RO membranes modification. The self-synthesized CDs enhanced the hydrophilicity of TFN membrane and reduced the thickness of the selective layer. The surface morphology of the CDs-TFN membrane exhibited a ridge-and-valley structure with uniform protrusions in the PSf substrate side and rougher surfaces compared with the pristine PA reverse osmosis membrane, which showed relatively smooth. The hydrophilic properties of CDs greatly assisted the interfacial polymerization which led to the formation of a dense separation layer. The TFN membrane with 0.02wt% CDs incorporation exhibited high water flux of 87.1L/(m2 h) and improved salt rejection of 98.8%, which outperformed state-of-the-art of commercial membrane. Results also show that optimizing the deposition time, is an important factor to achieving improved performance of the CDs-TFN membrane. This study demonstrated that CDs can provide an exciting direction in development of high-performance RO membranes in water desalination. Further improvement in desalination performance can be expected when incorporating chemically or physically modified CDs to enhance the membrane antifouling properties.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Key Project of International Cooperation, Bureau of International Co-operation, CAS. The authors are grateful to the reviewers for their helpful comments. The authors acknowledge Dr. Ruizhi Pang and Olusegun K. Abass for their assistance.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.memsci.2017.05.026.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: UNESCO, Managing Water under Uncertainty and Risk, in, Paris, France, 2012.
